# Gemini Review Pass 1: Fact Checker (RAG-Grounded)

> **You are a fact-checking reviewer for Ukrainian language curriculum.**
> Your ONLY task: compare the module content against authoritative reference sources.
> You do NOT evaluate style, pedagogy, or naturalness — that is a separate reviewer's job.

**IMPORTANT: This content was generated by another AI agent. You are an independent fact-checker.
Compare claims against the provided reference sources. Do NOT evaluate from your own knowledge.**

---

## Module Content

```markdown
## Вступ (Introduction)

Welcome to one of the most practical modules you'll encounter — telling time, naming days, and learning months in Ukrainian. By the end of this module, you'll be able to ask **«Котра́ годи́на?»** (What time is it?), understand the answer, and talk about your weekly schedule like a confident traveler.

Here's your motivation: **Укрзалізни́ця** — Ukraine's national railway — is famous for its punctuality. Trains across the country run on time, with a reported on-time rate of over 90%. When you're standing on a platform in Kyiv or Lviv, you need to read the departure board, understand announcements, and be **вча́сно** (on time). Being late for a Ukrainian train is not an option!

Imagine you're at the train station and you need to ask a stranger for the time. In Ukrainian, you'd use a polite form with the Vocative case — a form of address you've already seen in greetings like **па́не** and **па́ні**:

- **Па́не, ска́жіть, будь ла́ска, котра́ годи́на?** — Sir, please tell me, what time is it?
- **Па́ні, ска́жіть, будь ла́ска, котра́ годи́на?** — Ma'am, please tell me, what time is it?

> [!tip] Quick Win
> You already know **будь ла́ска** (please), **па́не/па́ні** (sir/ma'am), and numbers from the previous module. You're more prepared for this than you think!

That polite phrase — **ска́жіть, будь ла́ска** — works in any situation where you need information. It's your universal key to polite conversation in Ukraine.

Time vocabulary touches every part of daily life: catching trains, meeting friends, making plans. Let's start with how Ukrainians tell the hour.

## Презентація часу (Time Presentation)

### Asking for the Time

Ukrainian has two key time questions, and they're used in different situations:

- **Котра́ годи́на?** — What time is it? (right now)
- **О ко́трій?** — At what time? (for schedules, plans, events)

A conductor at the station might hear both:

> **(Вокзал / Train station)**
>
> — Па́не, котра́ годи́на?
> — За́раз во́сьма годи́на.
> — А о ко́трій по́їзд на Льві́в?
> — По́їзд о дев'я́тій п'ятна́дцять.

Notice how the first question asks about *now* (**за́раз** — now), while the second asks about a scheduled event with the preposition **о** (at).

### Telling the Hour

Here's the key rule: Ukrainian uses **ordinal numbers in the feminine gender** to tell the hour. Why feminine? Because **годи́на** (hour) is a feminine noun, so the number agrees with it.

| Hour | Ukrainian | Gloss |
|------|-----------|-------|
| 1:00 | пе́рша годи́на | first hour |
| 2:00 | дру́га годи́на | second hour |
| 3:00 | тре́тя годи́на | third hour |
| 4:00 | четве́рта годи́на | fourth hour |
| 5:00 | п'я́та годи́на | fifth hour |
| 6:00 | шо́ста годи́на | sixth hour |
| 7:00 | сьо́ма годи́на | seventh hour |
| 8:00 | во́сьма годи́на | eighth hour |
| 9:00 | дев'я́та годи́на | ninth hour |
| 10:00 | деся́та годи́на | tenth hour |
| 11:00 | одина́дцята годи́на | eleventh hour |
| 12:00 | двана́дцята годи́на | twelfth hour |

> [!warning] Common Mistake
> English says "It is **two** o'clock" using a cardinal number. Ukrainian says **«За́раз дру́га годи́на»** — using an **ordinal** number (second, not two). Never say ~~два годин~~ — that's a direct translation from English and it's wrong.

In casual speech, Ukrainians often drop **годи́на** when the context is clear:

- **За́раз дру́га.** — It's two (o'clock).
- **За́раз деся́та.** — It's ten (o'clock).

### Adding Minutes: Telegraphic Style

The simplest way to say minutes is the "telegraphic style" — just say the hour number followed by the minutes. This is what you'll see on train schedules and phone screens:

- **во́сьма три́дцять** — 8:30
- **дев'я́та п'ятна́дцять** — 9:15
- **деся́та со́рок п'я́ть** — 10:45

This style is perfectly natural and widely used. At A1, this is all you need for minutes.

> [!tip] Pro Tip
> You may hear **пів** (half) in expressions like **пів на дев'я́ту** (half to the ninth = 8:30). Don't worry about this pattern yet — the telegraphic style will get you through any conversation.

Let's see a quick dialogue using times with minutes:

> **(Перо́н / Platform)**
>
> — О ко́трій по́їзд на Ки́їв?
> — О деся́тій три́дцять.
> — Дя́кую! А за́раз котра́ годи́на?
> — За́раз деся́та п'ятна́дцять.

You're doing great! Now let's move on to days and months.

## Дні та місяці (Days and Months)

### Days of the Week

Ukrainian has seven days — **дні ти́жня** (days of the week). Here they are, starting from Monday, as is standard in Ukraine:

| Day | Ukrainian | Gloss |
|-----|-----------|-------|
| Monday | понеді́лок | after Sunday (неділя) |
| Tuesday | вівто́рок | the second (day) |
| Wednesday | середа́ | the middle |
| Thursday | четве́р | the fourth |
| Friday | п'я́тниця | the fifth |
| Saturday | субо́та | rest day |
| Sunday | неді́ля | no-work day |

> [!warning] False Friend Alert
> **Неді́ля** means **Sunday**, NOT "week"! The word for "week" is **ти́ждень**. This trips up many learners, so remember: **неді́ля** = Sunday, **ти́ждень** = week.

To say "on Monday," "on Tuesday," etc., Ukrainian uses **у/в** + the day name. Treat these as fixed phrases — just memorize them as chunks:

- **у понеді́лок** — on Monday
- **у вівто́рок** — on Tuesday
- **у сере́ду** — on Wednesday
- **у четве́р** — on Thursday
- **у п'я́тницю** — on Friday
- **у субо́ту** — on Saturday
- **у неді́лю** — on Sunday

> [!note] Why Do the Endings Change?
> You might notice that **середа** becomes **у сере́ду**, and **субо́та** becomes **у субо́ту**. These are Accusative case forms — but don't worry about the grammar label right now. Just learn each "on + day" phrase as a unit. The pattern will make more sense when you study cases in detail later.

A conductor checking the schedule might say:

> **(Вокза́л / Station)**
>
> — Ко́ли по́їзд на Оде́су?
> — У понеді́лок і в середу.
> — А у п'я́тницю?
> — Ні, у п'я́тницю по́їзд не хо́дить.

### Months and Seasons

Ukrainian has twelve months — **двана́дцять мі́сяців**. Unlike English, months are always written in **lowercase** in Ukrainian. They group naturally into four seasons:

**Зима́ (Winter):**
- гру́день — December
- сі́чень — January
- лю́тий — February

**Весна́ (Spring):**
- бе́резень — March
- кві́тень — April
- тра́вень — May

**Лі́то (Summer):**
- че́рвень — June
- ли́пень — July
- се́рпень — August

**О́сінь (Autumn):**
- ве́ресень — September
- жо́втень — October
- листопа́д — November

> [!culture] Ukrainian Months Tell a Story
> Ukrainian month names come from nature, not Latin emperors. **Бе́резень** (March) comes from **бере́за** (birch tree) — birch sap starts flowing. **Ли́пень** (July) comes from **ли́па** (linden tree) — it blooms in summer. **Листопа́д** (November) literally means "leaf-fall." These names connect you to the Ukrainian landscape.

To say "in January," "in March," etc., use **у/в** + the Locative form of the month:

- **у сі́чні** — in January
- **у лю́тому** — in February
- **у бе́резні** — in March
- **у кві́тні** — in April
- **у тра́вні** — in May
- **у че́рвні** — in June
- **у ли́пні** — in July
- **у се́рпні** — in August
- **у ве́ресні** — in September
- **у жо́втні** — in October
- **у листопа́ді** — in November
- **у гру́дні** — in December

> [!tip] Lowercase Rule
> In English you write "January" with a capital letter. In Ukrainian, months and days of the week are always lowercase: **сі́чень**, **понеді́лок**. Only capitalize them at the start of a sentence.

Here's how months come up in conversation:

- **У сі́чні хо́лодно.** — It's cold in January.
- **У ли́пні те́пло.** — It's warm in July.
- **Но́вий рі́к — пе́рше сі́чня.** — New Year is January first.

## Практика та розклад (Practice and Schedule)

### 12-Hour vs. 24-Hour Format

Ukrainian uses two systems for telling time, depending on the situation:

**Official (24-hour)** — for train schedules, announcements, digital clocks:
- **чотирна́дцята годи́на** — 14:00 (2 PM)
- **двадця́та три́дцять** — 20:30 (8:30 PM)

**Colloquial (12-hour)** — for everyday conversation with friends and family:
- **дру́га годи́на дня** — 2 PM (second hour of the day)
- **во́сьма годи́на ве́чора** — 8 PM (eighth hour of the evening)

The day divides into four parts:

- **ра́нок** (morning) — roughly 6:00–12:00
- **де́нь** (day/afternoon) — roughly 12:00–18:00
- **ве́чір** (evening) — roughly 18:00–22:00
- **ніч** (night) — roughly 22:00–6:00

So in casual speech, you'd clarify which part of the day:

- **Дев'я́та ра́нку.** — 9 in the morning.
- **Тре́тя дня.** — 3 in the afternoon.
- **Сьо́ма ве́чора.** — 7 in the evening.

### Prepositions for Planning

Three prepositions help you build a schedule:

| Preposition | Meaning | Example |
|-------------|---------|---------|
| **о** | at | **о дев'я́тій** — at nine |
| **до** | until / before | **до п'я́тої** — until five |
| **пі́сля** | after | **пі́сля шо́стої** — after six |

Let's put it all together with a conductor's daily **ро́зклад** (schedule):

> **(Ро́зклад провідни́ці / Conductor's schedule)**
>
> — О ко́трій ти працю́єш у понеді́лок?
> — Вра́нці, о во́сьмій.
> — А до ко́трої?
> — До п'я́тої ве́чора.
> — А у субо́ту?
> — У субо́ту я не працю́ю. Вихідни́й де́нь!

> [!practice] Your Turn!
> Try building your own schedule. Think about your week: **О ко́трій** do you wake up? What do you do **у понеді́лок**? **У субо́ту**? Use the patterns from this module to describe a typical day.

Let's see how all the pieces fit together — time, day, and month in one sentence:

- **По́їзд у понеді́лок о деся́тій.** — The train is on Monday at ten.
- **У ве́ресні шко́ла.** — School is in September.
- **Я працю́ю до п'я́тої.** — I work until five.

> [!note] Remember
> You don't need to be perfect. Ukrainians will understand you even if your endings aren't quite right. The important thing is to try. You already know numbers, and now you can use them with time — that's real progress!


```

## Plan (Source of Truth for Scope)

```yaml
module: a1-023
level: A1
sequence: 23
slug: what-time-is-it
version: '2.0'
title: What Time Is It?
subtitle: Telling Time, Days, and Months
focus: grammar
pedagogy: PPP
phase: A1.2 [Verbs & Sentences]
word_target: 1200
objectives:
- Learner can ask and tell the time
- Learner can name all days of the week
- Learner can name all months of the year
- Learner can use time prepositions correctly
content_outline:
- section: Вступ (Introduction)
  words: 250
  points:
  - 'Cultural hook: Ukrzaliznytsia (90%+ punctuality) as the backbone of Ukrainian resilience; learning to be «вчасно» (on
    time) as a core value.'
  - 'Communicative competence (Standard §4.2.3.4): Using the Vocative case and polite address forms: «Пане / пані, скажіть,
    будь ласка, котра година?».'
- section: Презентація часу (Time Presentation)
  words: 350
  points:
  - 'Asking for time: Distinction between «Котра година?» (current time) and «О котрій?» (scheduled time for events or train
    arrivals).'
  - 'Hour identification: Using ordinal numbers in feminine gender (перша, друга). Learner error correction: translate ''It
    is two o''clock'' as «Зараз друга година», not «два годин».'
  - 'A1 simplicity (Standard §3.4): Prioritize ''telegraphic style'' (вісім тридцять) for minutes before introducing complex
    fractions like «пів на» or «чверть».'
- section: Дні та місяці (Days and Months)
  words: 350
  points:
  - 'Days of the week: Grammar focus (§4.2.3.2.2) on «у/в» + Accusative case. Learner error: mimicking ''in/at'' with Locative
    (*у понеділку) instead of correct «у понеділок».'
  - 'Months and Seasons: Locative case (§4.2.3.3) for months (у січні). Note on orthography: unlike English, months and days
    are lowercase in Ukrainian.'
  - Associating months with weather and seasonal cycles in Ukraine to aid memorization.
- section: Практика та розклад (Practice and Schedule)
  words: 250
  points:
  - 'Register distinction: 24-hour format for official schedules (transport, digital) vs. 12-hour format for colloquial speech
    («друга година дня» vs. «чотирнадцята година»).'
  - 'Prepositions for planning: Using «о» (at), «до» (until), and «після» (after) to create a simple conductor''s shift schedule
    or a daily routine.'
vocabulary_hints:
  required:
  - година (hour) — котра година?, о першій годині, ціла година; High frequency (Core A1)
  - хвилина (minute) — за п'ять хвилин, одна хвилина, 30 хвилин; High frequency (Core A1)
  - день (day) — добрий день, робочий день, вихідний день; High frequency (Core A1)
  - тиждень (week) — на цьому тижні, наступного тижня, дні тижня; High frequency (Core A1)
  - місяць (month) — у цьому місяці, минулого місяця, зимові місяці; High frequency (Core A1)
  - рік (year) — новий рік, цього року, минулого року; High frequency (Core A1)
  - ранок (morning) — доброго ранку, о восьмій ранку, завтра вранці
  - вечір (evening) — добрий вечір, сьогодні ввечері, о сьомій вечора
  recommended:
  - вчасно (on time) — бути вчасно, прийти вчасно, поїзд прибув вчасно; Med frequency (A1/A2)
  - понеділок (Monday) — у понеділок, важкий понеділок; usage with Accusative
  - січень (January) — у січні, перше січня; usage with Locative
  - зараз (now)
  - пізно (late)
  - рано (early)
  - о котрій (at what time)
activity_hints:
- type: match-up
  focus: Match times to clock faces
  items: 20
- type: fill-in
  focus: Complete time expressions
  items: 20
- type: quiz
  focus: Order days and months
  items: 15
- type: fill-in
  focus: Schedule a meeting
  items: 6
connects_to:
- a1-46 (Can, Must, Want - Modals)
- a1-38 (My Daily Routine)
prerequisites:
- a1-22 (Numbers and Money)
persona:
  voice: Patient Supportive Tutor
  role: Ukrzaliznytsia Conductor
grammar:
- Clock time expressions (Котра година?)
- Time prepositions (о, до, після)
- Days and months in context
register: розмовний

```

## Activities YAML

```yaml
- type: match-up
  title: Match the Time
  instruction: Match each Ukrainian time expression to its digital equivalent.
  pairs:
    - left: "перша година"
      right: "1:00"
    - left: "друга година"
      right: "2:00"
    - left: "третя година"
      right: "3:00"
    - left: "п'ята година"
      right: "5:00"
    - left: "сьома година"
      right: "7:00"
    - left: "восьма година"
      right: "8:00"
    - left: "десята година"
      right: "10:00"
    - left: "дванадцята година"
      right: "12:00"
    - left: "восьма тридцять"
      right: "8:30"
    - left: "дев'ята п'ятнадцять"
      right: "9:15"

- type: fill-in
  title: Complete the Time Expression
  instruction: Fill in the missing word to complete each time expression.
  items:
    - sentence: "Зараз ___ година."
      answer: "перша"
      options: ["перша", "друга", "один", "першій"]
      explanation: "1:00 is перша година — use the feminine ordinal, not the cardinal number."
    - sentence: "Зараз ___ година."
      answer: "восьма"
      options: ["восьма", "вісім", "восьмій", "восьмого"]
      explanation: "8:00 is восьма година — feminine ordinal for the hour."
    - sentence: "___ котра година?"
      answer: "Зараз"
      options: ["Зараз", "Завтра", "Добре", "Тут"]
      explanation: "Зараз means now — used when stating the current time."
    - sentence: "О ___ поїзд на Львів?"
      answer: "котрій"
      options: ["котрій", "котра", "скільки", "який"]
      explanation: "О котрій? means At what time? — used for scheduled events."
    - sentence: "Поїзд о ___ тридцять."
      answer: "десятій"
      options: ["десятій", "десята", "десять", "десятого"]
      explanation: "With the preposition о (at), the hour takes a special ending — о десятій."
    - sentence: "Я працюю ___ п'ятої."
      answer: "до"
      options: ["до", "о", "після", "у"]
      explanation: "До means until — я працюю до п'ятої (I work until five)."
    - sentence: "___ шостої я вдома."
      answer: "Після"
      options: ["Після", "До", "О", "У"]
      explanation: "Після means after — після шостої (after six)."
    - sentence: "Дев'ята ___."
      answer: "ранку"
      options: ["ранку", "ранок", "вранці", "рано"]
      explanation: "Дев'ята ранку means 9 in the morning — ранку is the genitive form used with time."

- type: quiz
  title: Days, Months, and Time
  instruction: Choose the correct answer.
  items:
    - question: "Which day of the week does the Ukrainian week start with?"
      options:
        - text: "понеділок"
          correct: true
        - text: "неділя"
          correct: false
        - text: "субота"
          correct: false
        - text: "середа"
          correct: false
      explanation: "The Ukrainian week starts with понеділок (Monday), not Sunday."
    - question: "What does неділя mean?"
      options:
        - text: "Sunday"
          correct: true
        - text: "week"
          correct: false
        - text: "Saturday"
          correct: false
        - text: "holiday"
          correct: false
      explanation: "Неділя is a false friend — it means Sunday, not week. The word for week is тиждень."
    - question: "Which month name comes from береза (birch tree)?"
      options:
        - text: "березень"
          correct: true
        - text: "липень"
          correct: false
        - text: "листопад"
          correct: false
        - text: "вересень"
          correct: false
      explanation: "Березень (March) comes from береза (birch) — birch sap starts flowing in spring."
    - question: "What does листопад literally mean?"
      options:
        - text: "leaf-fall"
          correct: true
        - text: "snow-fall"
          correct: false
        - text: "rain-fall"
          correct: false
        - text: "wind-blow"
          correct: false
      explanation: "Листопад (November) literally means leaf-fall — лист (leaf) + пад (fall)."
    - question: "How do you say 'on Monday' in Ukrainian?"
      options:
        - text: "у понеділок"
          correct: true
        - text: "на понеділок"
          correct: false
        - text: "в понеділку"
          correct: false
        - text: "о понеділок"
          correct: false
      explanation: "Days of the week use у/в + the day name: у понеділок."
    - question: "How do you say 'in January' in Ukrainian?"
      options:
        - text: "у січні"
          correct: true
        - text: "у січень"
          correct: false
        - text: "в січня"
          correct: false
        - text: "на січень"
          correct: false
      explanation: "Months use у/в + the Locative form: у січні."
    - question: "Which word means 'on time' in Ukrainian?"
      options:
        - text: "вчасно"
          correct: true
        - text: "зараз"
          correct: false
        - text: "рано"
          correct: false
        - text: "пізно"
          correct: false
      explanation: "Вчасно means on time — бути вчасно (to be on time)."
    - question: "In Ukrainian, are months and days written with a capital or lowercase letter?"
      options:
        - text: "lowercase"
          correct: true
        - text: "capital letter"
          correct: false
        - text: "both are acceptable"
          correct: false
        - text: "capital only for months"
          correct: false
      explanation: "Unlike English, Ukrainian writes months and days in lowercase: січень, понеділок."

- type: fill-in
  title: Plan Your Schedule
  instruction: Complete each sentence with the correct word to build a weekly schedule.
  items:
    - sentence: "Поїзд ___ понеділок о десятій."
      answer: "у"
      options: ["у", "о", "на", "до"]
      explanation: "Days of the week use у/в: у понеділок (on Monday)."
    - sentence: "О котрій ти працюєш ___ середу?"
      answer: "у"
      options: ["у", "о", "на", "до"]
      explanation: "У середу means on Wednesday — same pattern as all days of the week."
    - sentence: "У суботу я не працюю. ___ день!"
      answer: "Вихідний"
      options: ["Вихідний", "Робочий", "Перший", "Останній"]
      explanation: "Вихідний день means a day off — у суботу вихідний день (Saturday is a day off)."
    - sentence: "У ___ холодно."
      answer: "січні"
      options: ["січні", "січень", "січня", "січню"]
      explanation: "In January uses the Locative form: у січні."
    - sentence: "Я працюю о восьмій ___ до п'ятої вечора."
      answer: "ранку"
      options: ["ранку", "ранок", "рано", "вранці"]
      explanation: "О восьмій ранку means at eight in the morning."
    - sentence: "У ___ тепло."
      answer: "липні"
      options: ["липні", "липень", "липня", "липню"]
      explanation: "In July uses the Locative form: у липні."

- type: true-false
  title: True or False?
  instruction: Decide if each statement about Ukrainian time is true or false.
  items:
    - statement: "In Ukrainian, you say the hour using ordinal numbers like перша, друга, третя."
      correct: true
      explanation: "Ukrainian uses feminine ordinal numbers for hours because година is feminine."
    - statement: "To say 2:00 in Ukrainian, you say два година."
      correct: false
      explanation: "Never use cardinal numbers for time. Say друга година (second hour), not два година."
    - statement: "Восьма тридцять means 8:30."
      correct: true
      explanation: "This is the telegraphic style — just the hour ordinal followed by minutes."
    - statement: "The word тиждень means Sunday."
      correct: false
      explanation: "Тиждень means week. Sunday is неділя."
    - statement: "The preposition о means 'at' when talking about time."
      correct: true
      explanation: "О котрій? (At what time?) and о восьмій (at eight) use о for scheduled times."
    - statement: "In Ukrainian, months are written with a capital letter like in English."
      correct: false
      explanation: "Ukrainian writes months and days in lowercase: січень, лютий, понеділок."
    - statement: "Середа means Wednesday, because it is the middle of the week."
      correct: true
      explanation: "Середа comes from середина (middle) — it is the middle day of the work week."
    - statement: "Ранок, день, вечір, ніч are the four parts of a Ukrainian day."
      correct: true
      explanation: "Morning, daytime, evening, night — used to clarify 12-hour time (дев'ята ранку)."

- type: group-sort
  title: Sort the Months by Season
  instruction: Place each month into the correct season.
  groups:
    - name: "Зима (Winter)"
      items: ["грудень", "січень", "лютий"]
    - name: "Весна (Spring)"
      items: ["березень", "квітень", "травень"]
    - name: "Літо (Summer)"
      items: ["червень", "липень", "серпень"]
    - name: "Осінь (Autumn)"
      items: ["вересень", "жовтень", "листопад"]

- type: match-up
  title: Match the Day to Its Meaning
  instruction: Match each Ukrainian day of the week to its English equivalent.
  pairs:
    - left: "понеділок"
      right: "Monday"
    - left: "вівторок"
      right: "Tuesday"
    - left: "середа"
      right: "Wednesday"
    - left: "четвер"
      right: "Thursday"
    - left: "п'ятниця"
      right: "Friday"
    - left: "субота"
      right: "Saturday"
    - left: "неділя"
      right: "Sunday"

- type: unjumble
  title: Put the Words in Order
  instruction: Arrange the words to form a correct Ukrainian sentence about time or schedule.
  items:
    - words: ["година", "Зараз", "восьма"]
      answer: "Зараз восьма година"
    - words: ["котра", "година", "Скажіть,"]
      answer: "Скажіть, котра година"
    - words: ["поїзд", "О", "котрій"]
      answer: "О котрій поїзд"
    - words: ["понеділок", "працюю", "Я", "у"]
      answer: "Я працюю у понеділок"
    - words: ["холодно", "січні", "У"]
      answer: "У січні холодно"
    - words: ["п'ятої", "до", "працюю", "Я"]
      answer: "Я працюю до п'ятої"
```

---

## Research Notes

# Дослідження: What Time Is It?

## State Standard Reference
§4.2.3.2 (Accusative case — days of the week: «у понеділок»)
§4.2.3.3 (Locative case — months: «у січні»)
§4.2.3.4 (Vocative — polite address: «Пане / пані, скажіть, будь ласка...»)
Alignment: Module covers three intersecting A1 grammar points — ordinal numerals in clock time, Accusative for days-of-week temporal expressions, and Locative for month expressions. All three are within A1 scope per the quick-ref (Accusative from M25 — this is M23, so plan is slightly pre-scope for Accusative; treat days-of-week as frozen phrases rather than full case analysis).

> **Note for content writer**: Since Accusative case is formally introduced at M25, treat «у понеділок / у вівторок» etc. as memorized collocations (frozen time phrases), not case-analyzed forms. No grammar label needed.

---

## Vocabulary Frequency

| Word | Notes | Key collocations |
|------|-------|------------------|
| **годи́на** | Core A1 — highest-frequency time word | котра́ годи́на?, о пе́ршій годи́ні, пів годи́ни |
| **хвили́на** | Core A1 — essential for partial-hour times | за п'ять хвили́н, три́дцять хвили́н, одна хвили́на |
| **де́нь** | Polysemous: "day" and "afternoon" | до́брий де́нь, робо́чий де́нь, ці́лий де́нь |
| **ти́ждень** | Irregular declension (stem тижн-) | на цьо́му ти́жні, дні ти́жня, насту́пного ти́жня |
| **мі́сяць** | Also means "moon" — cultural note | у цьо́му мі́сяці, зимо́ві мі́сяці, мину́лого мі́сяця |
| **рі́к** | Irregular plural (ро́ки/роки́) — only base form needed at A1 | Но́вий рі́к, цього ро́ку, мину́лого ро́ку |
| **ра́нок** | Morning period; frozen adverb: вра́нці | до́брого ра́нку, о во́сьмій ра́нку, за́втра вра́нці |
| **ве́чір** | Evening period; frozen adverb: вве́чері | до́брий ве́чір, сього́дні вве́чері, о сьо́мій ве́чора |
| **за́раз** | High-frequency adverb, no inflection | За́раз дру́га годи́на. |
| **вча́сно** | Culturally significant (trains, punctuality) | бу́ти вча́сно, по́їзд прибу́в вча́сно |

---

## Cultural Hooks

1. **Ukrzaliznytsia punctuality**: Ukrainian state railways (Укрзалізниця) have a publicly quoted on-time rate of ~90%+. The conductor announcing «Поїзд прибуває о дев'ятій п'ятнадцять. Будьте вчасно!» is a real, recognizable scenario — ties the persona directly to lived Ukrainian experience.

2. **New Year (Новий рік) vs. Christmas timing**: In Ukraine, the secular New Year (1 січня) is the major winter celebration, while Christmas is observed on 7 січня (Orthodox) and 25 грудня (Greek Catholic / newer tradition). This gives authentic context for months and dates: «Перше січня — Новий рік. Сьоме січня — Різдво.»

3. **Working week rhythm**: Ukrainian workweek is Monday–Friday (понеділок–п'ятниця); weekend is субота і неділя. Note: «неділя» = Sunday (NOT "week" — that is «тиждень»). This is a common false-friend trap for learners.

---

## Common Learner Errors

1. **«два годин» → «дру́га годи́на»** — Clock hours use ordinal numerals in feminine gender (перша, друга, третя...), not cardinal numbers (один, два). English "It is two o'clock" maps to «Зараз друга година», not *«зараз два годин».

2. **Locative for days: «*у понеділку»→ «у понеділок»** — English "on Monday" triggers interference; learners expect Locative (location sense) but days-of-week take Accusative. Treat as frozen phrase to avoid case overload at A1.

3. **«неділя» ≠ week** — English speakers see "неділя" and guess "week" (cf. "sundial"/"Sunday" confusion). «Тиждень» = week; «неділя» = Sunday. Worth an explicit call-out.

4. **Lowercase months/days** — Unlike English, Ukrainian месяці and дні тижня are always lowercase: «у середу», «в березні». Learners default to capitalizing.

---

## Cross-References
- **Builds on**: a1-22 (Numbers and Money) — ordinal numerals and counting are prerequisites
- **Prepares for**: a1-24 (Can, Must, Want — Modals), a1-38 (My Daily Routine) — daily routine requires time-telling as scaffolding

---

## Teaching Strategy

- **Discovery exercise**: Show learners two train tickets side by side:
  - Ticket A: «Відправлення: 14:30» → «Поїзд відправляється о чотирнадцятій тридцять»
  - Ticket B: Clock face showing 2:30 PM → «Зараз друга тридцять»
  Ask: "How does the conductor say the time officially? How do friends say it?" Learners notice the 12h/24h register split before any rule is stated.
- **Best context**: Train station / Ukrzaliznytsia schedule board — a departure board lists «Київ → Львів, 08:15, понеділок» giving hours, minutes, and day-of-week in a single authentic artifact.
- **Contrastive pairs**:
  - «Котра́ годи́на?» (What time is it now?) vs. «О ко́трій?» (At what time does it happen?)
  - «у поне́ділок» (on Monday — Accusative frozen phrase) vs. «у сі́чні» (in January — Locative)
  - 12h colloquial: «дру́га годи́на дня» vs. 24h official: «чоти́рнадцята годи́на»

---

## Notes for Content Writing

- The Ukrzaliznytsia conductor persona is a strong scaffold — use it consistently across all section examples (conductor announcing times, reading schedules, greeting passengers).
- For minutes: **telegraphic style first** («вісім тридцять», «дев'ять п'ятнадцять»). Introduce «пів» (half) as a bonus; omit «чверть» and «за» constructions at A1.
- Days of week: give all 7 with «у/в» forms but label them as fixed phrases, not a case exercise.
- Months: give all 12 in a table with «у + Locative» forms and seasonal grouping (зима, весна, літо, осінь).
- Stress marks are mandatory on all bolded Ukrainian vocabulary.

## Friction Constraints (Past Review Findings)

FRICTION CONSTRAINTS (from past build reviews — DO NOT repeat these errors):
- [GLOBAL] сес-тра is a VALID word division per Правопис 2019 §49. Do NOT mark it as an error. Phonetic syllabification (се-стра) and typographic word division (сес-тра) follow different rules — both are correct in their respective contexts.
- [GLOBAL] Ukrainian textbooks teach a hands-on-EARS test for voicing (закрий долонями вуха), NOT a hand-on-throat test. The hand-on-throat test is a valid phonetics technique but must NOT be attributed to Ukrainian textbooks. Source: Кравцова 2019, Grade 2, p.39.
- [GLOBAL] Do NOT invent Ukrainian words for minimal pairs. "Сір" is NOT a word meaning "grey" — the correct form is "сірий". Use verified minimal pairs only: кит/кіт, бити/біти, лис/ліс.

---

## Your Task: Plan Adherence + Factual Verification

### PART A: Plan Adherence Check (MANDATORY)

The plan YAML above is the source of truth for what the module MUST contain. Check EVERY point systematically:

#### A1. Content Outline Points

For EACH section in `content_outline`, and for EACH `points` entry within that section:
1. Search the module content for evidence that this point is addressed
2. Classify as:
   - **COVERED** — the point is clearly addressed in the content. Quote the relevant sentence(s).
   - **MISSING** — the point does not appear in the content. This is a **MEDIUM** severity issue.
   - **PARTIAL** — some aspect is covered but key details are missing. Note what's missing.

**Output a checklist:**
```
### Plan Point Coverage
- Section "Вступ — Introduction":
  - [x] Point 1: "A1 immersion 10-50%..." — COVERED (line ~5: "...")
  - [ ] Point 2: "Cultural hook: Cyrillic script..." — MISSING
  ...
```

#### A2. Required Vocabulary

For EACH word in `vocabulary_hints.required`:
1. Search the module content (not just the word list section — the entire prose)
2. Check if it appears in the activities YAML (if provided in the content above)
3. Classify as:
   - **PRESENT** — found in prose AND activities
   - **PROSE ONLY** — in prose but not in any activity (LOW)
   - **MISSING** — not in prose at all (**HIGH** severity)

#### A3. Activity Hints

For EACH entry in `activity_hints`:
1. Check if an activity of that `type` exists
2. Check if the `focus` matches
3. Check if the `items` count is met
4. Flag missing activity types as **MEDIUM**

#### A4. Section Word Budgets

For EACH section in `content_outline` with a `words` target:
1. Estimate the word count of the corresponding content section
2. Flag sections >10% under their target as **MEDIUM**

#### A5. Pronunciation Videos (if plan has `pronunciation_videos`)

Check that video links from the plan are embedded in the content. Flag missing embeds as **MEDIUM**.

---

### PART B: Factual Verification

For EVERY grammar explanation, factual claim, cultural assertion, and historical reference in the module content:

1. **Search the reference sources above** for confirming or contradicting evidence
2. **Classify each claim** as one of:
   - **CONFIRMED [Tier 1]** — matches a reference source. Cite the source (chunk ID or source identifier) and quote both the module claim and the reference.
   - **DISCREPANCY [Tier 1]** — contradicts a reference source. HIGH severity. Cite both the module claim and the contradicting reference with chunk ID. Suggest the correct version.
   - **UNVERIFIED** — no reference coverage. Flag explicitly. Do NOT evaluate from your own knowledge. Do NOT mark as correct or incorrect — just "UNVERIFIED".

### Citation Enforcement

Every DISCREPANCY entry MUST include:
- Exact quote from the module content
- Exact quote from the reference source
- **Chunk ID or source identifier** (e.g., "Textbook, Grade 3, chunk_abc123" or "VESUM: lemma X")

Free-text citations with no chunk ID or source identifier carry ZERO weight and will be programmatically discarded.

### Special Checks

1. **Callout boxes** (`[!did-you-know]`, `[!myth-buster]`, `[!culture-note]`, `[!fun-fact]`): These are the #1 source of fabrication. Scrutinize every claim.
2. **Grammar rules**: Verify rule statements against textbook references. Flag overgeneralizations.
3. **Etymologies**: Verify word origins against references. Flag folk etymologies.
4. **Statistics and dates**: Flag unsourced numbers.
5. **VESUM failures**: Words not found in VESUM may be misspellings or Russianisms.

---

## Output Format

You MUST use these exact delimiters:

```
===FACTUAL_REVIEW_START===
## Plan Adherence Summary

**Content outline points:** N total, N covered, N missing, N partial
**Required vocabulary:** N total, N present, N missing
**Activity hints:** N total, N matched, N missing
**Plan Adherence Score:** X/10
(10 = all points covered; deduct 1 per MISSING content point, 2 per MISSING required word)

### Plan Point Coverage

[Checklist from Part A1 — every point with [x] or [ ] and evidence]

### Required Vocabulary Coverage

| Word | In Prose? | In Activities? | Status |
|------|-----------|---------------|--------|
| ... | YES/NO | YES/NO | PRESENT / PROSE ONLY / MISSING |

### Activity Hints Coverage

| Hint Type | Focus | Required Items | Found? | Actual Items |
|-----------|-------|---------------|--------|-------------|
| ... | ... | N | YES/NO | N |

### Missing Plan Points (Fix Targets)

[List each MISSING or PARTIAL point with the exact plan text and which section it belongs to.
These become fix targets in the SECTION_FIX block below.]

---

## Factual Verification Summary

**Total claims checked:** N
**Confirmed [Tier 1]:** N
**Discrepancies [Tier 1]:** N
**Unverified:** N
**Factual Alignment Score:** X/10

## Confirmed Claims

[List confirmed claims with source citations]

## Discrepancies

### Discrepancy 1: [brief title]
- **Module says:** "[exact quote from module]"
- **Reference says:** "[exact quote from reference source]"
- **Source:** [chunk ID / source identifier]
- **Severity:** HIGH
- **Suggested fix:** [what to change]

[... more discrepancies ...]

## Unverified Claims

[List claims with no reference coverage]

## Verdict

**Status:** PASS / FAIL
(FAIL if any HIGH severity discrepancy OR 3+ MISSING plan points found)
===FACTUAL_REVIEW_END===
```

If you find fixable discrepancies, also output FIND/REPLACE pairs:

```
===SECTION_FIX_START===
FILE: /Users/krisztiankoos/projects/learn-ukrainian/curriculum/l2-uk-en/a1/what-time-is-it.md
FIND:
[exact text from module with the error]
REPLACE:
[corrected text based on reference source]
---
[... more pairs, max 15 ...]
===SECTION_FIX_END===
```

---

## Boundaries

- Do NOT evaluate style, naturalness, or pedagogy — that is Pass 2's job
- Do NOT score dimensions (except Plan Adherence and Factual Alignment) — that is Pass 2's job
- Do NOT evaluate factual claims from your own knowledge — ONLY compare against provided references
- Plan adherence checking uses the plan YAML as ground truth — this is objective, not subjective
- Do NOT fabricate references — if no reference covers a claim, mark it UNVERIFIED
- Maximum 15 FIND/REPLACE pairs (prioritize MISSING plan points over minor factual fixes)
